400	/// `std::sys::cmath::*` names we allow in device code and rewrite to GPU math.
401	///
402	/// When you call `x.tan()` (also `atan`, `acos`, `cbrt`, the hyperbolics,
403	/// `exp_m1`, `ln_1p`, `hypot`, ...), the compiler turns it into a call to a
404	/// tiny `std` wrapper like `std::sys::cmath::tan`, which on a CPU forwards to
405	/// the system C math library. The GPU has no such library, and device code
406	/// may not call into `std`, so our "no std on the GPU" guard would normally
407	/// reject the call.
408	///
409	/// We never actually run `std` here: mir-importer rewrites each of these
410	/// names to the matching NVIDIA libdevice function (`__nv_tan`, `__nv_sinh`,
411	/// ...). This list just tells the guard "these are fine, we handle them."
412	/// Keep it in sync with the `std::sys::cmath` matches in float_math.rs.
413	///
414	/// A few functions (`sin`, `cos`, `exp`, ...) take a different, allowed route
415	/// on this toolchain: the compiler lowers them to a builtin in `core`, so
416	/// they never reach here. The ones below still go through `std` because they
417	/// are not part of `core_float_math`; `sin`/`cos` are listed defensively in
418	/// case a build ever takes the `std` route too. Only `std::`-prefixed names
419	/// belong here; `core`-based shims are already allowed.
420	fn is_intrinsic_lowered_cmath_shim(fn_path: &str) -> bool {
421	    matches!(
422	        fn_path,
423	        "std::sys::cmath::sinf"
424	            | "std::sys::cmath::sin"
425	            | "std::sys::cmath::cosf"
426	            | "std::sys::cmath::cos"
427	            | "std::sys::cmath::tanf"
428	            | "std::sys::cmath::tan"
429	            | "std::sys::cmath::asinf"
430	            | "std::sys::cmath::asin"
431	            | "std::sys::cmath::acosf"
432	            | "std::sys::cmath::acos"
433	            | "std::sys::cmath::atan2f"
434	            | "std::sys::cmath::atan2"
435	            | "std::sys::cmath::atanf"
436	            | "std::sys::cmath::atan"
437	            | "std::sys::cmath::cbrtf"
438	            | "std::sys::cmath::cbrt"
439	            | "std::sys::cmath::sinhf"
440	            | "std::sys::cmath::sinh"
441	            | "std::sys::cmath::coshf"
442	            | "std::sys::cmath::cosh"
443	            | "std::sys::cmath::tanhf"
444	            | "std::sys::cmath::tanh"
445	            | "std::sys::cmath::expm1f"
446	            | "std::sys::cmath::expm1"
447	            | "std::sys::cmath::log1pf"
448	            | "std::sys::cmath::log1p"
449	            | "std::sys::cmath::hypotf"
450	            | "std::sys::cmath::hypot"
451	    )
452	}
453	
454	/// Returns true for hidden `cuda_device::ptx_asm!` marker functions.
455	///
456	/// These markers have host-side `unreachable!()` bodies, but the MIR importer
457	/// rewrites their call sites to `nvvm.inline_ptx`. Collecting the marker body
458	/// itself would incorrectly pull panic-message machinery into device code.
459	fn is_ptx_asm_marker_path(fn_path: &str) -> bool {
460	    fn has_arity_suffix(path: &str, prefix: &str) -> bool {
461	        path.strip_prefix(prefix)
462	            .is_some_and(|suffix| suffix.parse::<usize>().is_ok())
463	    }
464	
465	    has_arity_suffix(fn_path, "cuda_device::ptx::__ptx_asm_out_")
466	        || has_arity_suffix(fn_path, "cuda_device::ptx::__ptx_asm_void_")
467	}
468	
469	/// Returns true for the hidden `__unroll_config::<FACTOR>` marker function.
470	///
471	/// `#[unroll]` / `#[unroll(N)]` on a loop makes the `#[kernel]` or `#[device]`
472	/// macro plant a call to this marker at the top of the loop body. The MIR
473	/// importer rewrites that call to a `mir.unroll_hint` op (consumed by the
474	/// loop-unroll pass) and never emits a real call. So the marker's empty body must
475	/// not be collected, or it would show up as a dead `.func` in the generated PTX.
476	/// Matches both the re-exported path (`cuda_device::__unroll_config`) and the
477	/// full path.
478	fn is_unroll_marker_path(fn_path: &str) -> bool {
479	    fn_path.contains("::__unroll_config")
480	}
481	
482	/// Marker substring of the panic message used by the public
483	/// `cuda_device::thread::index_*` stubs (see `cuda-device/src/thread.rs`).
484	///
485	/// Those public items exist only so imports resolve; real call sites
486	/// inside `#[kernel]` / `#[device]` bodies are rewritten by the proc
487	/// macros to `thread::__internal::*`. When this message shows up in
488	/// device-reachable MIR, a stub was reached through code that the macros
489	/// never rewrote, which means a helper function is missing `#[device]`.
490	const MISSING_DEVICE_STUB_MARKER: &str = "called outside #[kernel] / #[device]";
491	
492	/// Returns true for the panic entry points in `core` (and the `std`
493	/// re-export) that mark a basic block as a panic path.
494	///
495	/// Kept in sync with `is_unreachable_body`, which uses the same test to
496	/// recognize intrinsic placeholder bodies.
497	fn is_panic_entry_path(fn_path: &str) -> bool {
498	    fn_path.contains("::panicking::") || fn_path.contains("::rt::panic")
499	}
500	
501	/// If `fn_path` names one of the Rust global-allocator entry points,
502	/// returns the bare shim name (for use in the diagnostic), else `None`.
503	///
504	/// Every heap allocation (`Vec`, `Box`, `String`, ...) eventually funnels
505	/// into one of these. They have no MIR body (they are resolved by the
506	/// linker against the program's `#[global_allocator]`), and no device-side
507	/// allocator exists, so reaching one from a kernel can never work. This
508	/// list is the single switch point to revisit if a device allocator ever
509	/// lands.
510	fn rust_alloc_shim_name(fn_path: &str) -> Option<&str> {
511	    const SHIMS: [&str; 6] = [
512	        "__rust_alloc",
513	        "__rust_alloc_zeroed",
514	        "__rust_dealloc",
515	        "__rust_realloc",
516	        "__rust_no_alloc_shim_is_unstable_v2",
517	        "handle_alloc_error",
518	    ];
519	    let last = fn_path.rsplit("::").next().unwrap_or(fn_path);
520	    // None of these names is actually reserved: a user may legally define
521	    // their own `__rust_alloc` or `handle_alloc_error`, and such functions
522	    // compile for the device like any other. So a bare-name match is not
523	    // enough; the path must also come from the sysroot allocator machinery
524	    // (`alloc::alloc::*`, or the `std::alloc::*` re-export spelling). The
525	    // caller additionally skips local definitions by `DefId`.
526	    let matched = SHIMS.iter().find(|s| **s == last).copied()?;
527	    if !(fn_path.starts_with("alloc::") || fn_path.starts_with("std::alloc::")) {
528	        return None;
529	    }
530	    Some(matched)
531	}
532	
533	/// If `constant` is a `&str` constant, returns its text.
534	///
535	/// Used to inspect panic message strings in device-reachable MIR. Only
536	/// fat-pointer string constants are inspected; everything else returns
537	/// `None`. Evaluation failures (e.g. a constant in still-generic MIR)
538	/// also return `None`, which simply means "no message found".
539	fn const_str_text<'tcx>(tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, constant: &ConstOperand<'tcx>) -> Option<String> {
540	    let ty = constant.const_.ty();
541	    let TyKind::Ref(_, pointee, _) = ty.kind() else {
542	        return None;
543	    };
544	    if !matches!(pointee.kind(), TyKind::Str) {
545	        return None;
546	    }
547	    let val = constant
548	        .const_
549	        .eval(tcx, TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized(), constant.span)
550	        .ok()?;
551	    // `try_get_slice_bytes_for_diagnostics` ICEs on scalar / zero-sized
552	    // values, so only call it for the representations a `&str` constant
553	    // can actually have.
554	    if !matches!(val, ConstValue::Slice { .. } | ConstValue::Indirect { .. }) {
555	        return None;
556	    }
557	    let bytes = val.try_get_slice_bytes_for_diagnostics(tcx)?;
558	    Some(String::from_utf8_lossy(bytes).into_owned())
559	}
560	
561	/// Extracts the stub function name out of a stub panic message.
562	///
563	/// The message reads "internal error: entered unreachable code:
564	/// thread::index_1d called outside #[kernel] / #[device] ...", so the
565	/// stub name is the last word before " called outside".
566	fn stub_name_from_marker_message(text: &str) -> &str {
567	    text.split(" called outside")
568	        .next()
569	        .and_then(|prefix| prefix.rsplit(' ').next())
570	        .filter(|name| !name.is_empty())
571	        .unwrap_or("thread::index_*")
572	}
573	
574	/// Maps a MIR `SourceInfo` back to the span the user wrote.
575	///
576	/// The MIR inliner copies callee statements (with their callee-file
577	/// spans) into the caller and records the original call site in the
578	/// source-scope tree instead of in the span itself. Walking the chain of
579	/// inlined scopes recovers the outermost call site, the one that lives in
580	/// this body's own source; `source_callsite()` then additionally unwinds
581	/// any macro expansions (`vec!`, `panic!`, ...) sitting on top of it.
582	fn outermost_user_span<'tcx>(
583	    mir: &rustc_middle::mir::Body<'tcx>,
584	    source_info: rustc_middle::mir::SourceInfo,
585	) -> Span {
586	    let mut span = source_info.span;
587	    let mut scope = Some(source_info.scope);
588	    while let Some(s) = scope {
589	        let data = &mir.source_scopes[s];
590	        if let Some((_, callsite)) = data.inlined {
591	            span = callsite;
592	        }
593	        scope = data.inlined_parent_scope;
594	    }
595	    span.source_callsite()
596	}
597	
598	/// MIR visitor that records every `&str` constant in the visited range,
599	/// together with where it appeared (statement vs. terminator) and its
600	/// source span.
601	struct StrConstScan<'tcx> {
602	    tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,
603	    found: Vec<(Location, Span, String)>,
604	}
605	
606	impl<'tcx> Visitor<'tcx> for StrConstScan<'tcx> {
607	    fn visit_const_operand(&mut self, constant: &ConstOperand<'tcx>, location: Location) {
608	        if let Some(text) = const_str_text(self.tcx, constant) {
609	            self.found.push((location, constant.span, text));
610	        }
611	        self.super_const_operand(constant, location);
612	    }
613	}
614	
615	/// Provenance of a function discovered during the call-graph walk:
616	/// which root (kernel or standalone device fn) the walk started from, and
617	/// the nearest call site that still lives in user-written code.
618	///
619	/// Sysroot-internal call chains (e.g. `Box::new` -> `box_new_uninit` ->
620	/// `Global::alloc_impl` -> `__rust_alloc`) carry spans that point into
621	/// the standard library sources, which is exactly the kind of span the
622	/// inscrutable historic errors exposed. Propagating the last user-code
623	/// span along each discovery edge lets diagnostics point at the line in
624	/// the kernel that started the chain instead.
625	#[derive(Clone)]
626	struct DiscoveryCtx {
627	    /// Export name of the root the walk started from.
628	    root_name: String,
629	    /// True when the root is a `#[kernel]` entry point (as opposed to a
630	    /// standalone `#[device]` function).
631	    root_is_kernel: bool,
632	    /// Nearest enclosing user-code span on the discovery path.
633	    user_span: Span,
634	}
635	
636	/// Collects all device-reachable functions starting from kernel entry points.
637	///
638	/// This is the main entry point for device function collection. It:
639	///
640	/// 1. Finds all kernel entry points in the CGUs
641	/// 2. Walks the call graph from each kernel
642	/// 3. Returns all functions that need to be compiled to PTX
643	///
644	/// ## Parameters
645	///
646	/// - `tcx`: The type context containing all MIR bodies
647	/// - `cgus`: Codegen units from `tcx.collect_and_partition_mono_items()`
648	/// - `verbose`: If true, prints collection progress to stderr
649	///
650	/// ## Returns
651	///
652	/// A `CollectionResult` containing:
653	/// - `functions`: Collected functions with MIR bodies (kernels first, then callees)
654	/// - `device_externs`: External device function declarations (for FFI with external LTOIR)
655	pub fn collect_device_functions<'tcx>(
656	    tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,
657	    cgus: &[CodegenUnit<'tcx>],
658	    verbose: bool,
659	) -> CollectionResult<'tcx> {
660	    let mut collector = DeviceCollector::new(tcx, verbose);
661	
662	    // Find all kernel entry points
663	    for cgu in cgus {
664	        for (item, _data) in cgu.items() {
665	            if let MonoItem::Fn(instance) = item
666	                && is_kernel_function(tcx, instance.def_id())
667	            {
668	                // Skip closures inside kernels - they are device functions, not kernels.
669	                // Closures have names like "cuda_oxide_kernel_<hash>_foo::{closure#0}" but
670	                // only "cuda_oxide_kernel_<hash>_foo" is the actual kernel entry point.
671	                let name = tcx.def_path_str(instance.def_id());
672	                if name.contains("{closure") || name.contains("::closure") {
673	                    if verbose {
674	                        eprintln!(
675	                            "[collector] Skipping closure inside kernel (not an entry point): {}",
676	                            name
677	                        );
678	                    }
679	                    continue;
680	                }
681	
682	                // Skip generic (non-monomorphized) instances.
683	                // For generic kernels like scale<T>, the CGU contains both:
684	                // - The generic definition (scale<T>) - skip this
685	                // - Concrete instantiations (scale::<f32>) - process this
686	                if !is_fully_monomorphized(tcx, *instance) {
687	                    if verbose {
688	                        let name = tcx.def_path_str(instance.def_id());
689	                        eprintln!(
690	                            "[collector] Skipping non-monomorphized kernel: {} (needs type/const specialization)",
691	                            name
692	                        );
693	                    }
694	                    continue;
695	                }
696	
697	                let name = tcx.def_path_str(instance.def_id());
698	                // Extract the kernel base name by stripping the reserved
699	                // `cuda_oxide_kernel_<hash>_` prefix. Cross-crate kernels look
700	                // like `kernel_lib::cuda_oxide_kernel_<hash>_scale`; the
701	                // helper handles both bare and FQDN forms uniformly.
702	                let base_name = kernel_base_name(&name)
703	                    .map(str::to_string)
704	                    .unwrap_or_else(|| name.rsplit("::").next().unwrap_or(&name).to_string());
705	
706	                // Compute a unique export name for this kernel monomorphization.
707	                // Non-generic kernels keep the base name (e.g. "vecadd").
708	                // Type/const-generic kernels (including closure-generic) get
709	                // "<base>_TID_<hex32>", where <hex32> is the hash of the
710	                // concrete kernel function-item type. The host-side
711	                // `ptx_name()` emitted by `#[kernel]` / `#[cuda_module]`
712	                // computes the same string from the same `FnDef` type.
713	                let export_name = compute_kernel_export_name(tcx, *instance, &base_name);
714	
715	                if verbose {
716	                    eprintln!("[collector] Found kernel: {} -> {}", name, export_name);
717	                }
718	
719	                collector.add_root(*instance, true, export_name);
720	            }
721	        }
722	    }
723	
724	    // Find standalone device function roots (Phase 2: device functions without kernels).
725	    // Only scan when there are NO kernels — when kernels exist, device functions are
726	    // already collected transitively via the call graph walk.
727	    if collector.worklist.is_empty() {
728	        for cgu in cgus {
729	            for (item, _data) in cgu.items() {
730	                if let MonoItem::Fn(instance) = item
731	                    && is_device_function(tcx, instance.def_id())
732	                    && is_fully_monomorphized(tcx, *instance)
733	                {
734	                    let raw_name = tcx.def_path_str(instance.def_id());
735	
736	                    // Skip closures inside device functions
737	                    if raw_name.contains("{closure") || raw_name.contains("::closure") {
738	                        continue;
739	                    }
740	
741	                    // Use FQDN so the export name matches what the MIR translator
742	                    // sees via `CrateDef::name()` on the call side. The lowering
743	                    // layer converts `::` to `__` on both sides.
744	                    let name = collector.fqdn(*instance);
745	                    let export_name = collector.compute_export_name(&name, *instance);
746	
747	                    if verbose {
748	                        eprintln!(
749	                            "[collector] Found standalone device function: {} -> {}",
750	                            name, export_name
751	                        );
752	                    }
753	
754	                    // Add as a non-kernel root — produces .func (not .entry) in PTX
755	                    collector.add_root(*instance, false, export_name);
756	                }
757	            }
758	        }
759	    }
760	
761	    // Process the worklist to collect all reachable functions
762	    collector.collect()
763	}
764	
765	/// Worklist-based collector for device-reachable functions.
766	///
767	/// Uses breadth-first traversal to discover all functions reachable from kernels.
768	/// This ensures we don't miss any callees, even through deep call chains.
769	struct DeviceCollector<'tcx> {
770	    tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,
771	    /// Mangled names of functions already seen (prevents duplicates and infinite loops).
772	    /// We use mangled names because they uniquely identify each monomorphization,
773	    /// unlike DefId which is shared across all instantiations of a generic function.
774	    seen: HashSet<String>,
775	    /// Export names already used (prevents name conflicts in PTX).
776	    used_export_names: HashSet<String>,
777	    /// Functions awaiting processing.
778	    worklist: VecDeque<CollectedFunction<'tcx>>,
779	    /// Discovery provenance per collected function, keyed by mangled
780	    /// symbol name (same key as `seen`). Used by diagnostics to name the
781	    /// originating kernel and to point at user code instead of sysroot
782	    /// internals.
783	    discovery: HashMap<String, DiscoveryCtx>,
784	    /// Functions collected so far, in discovery order.
785	    result: Vec<CollectedFunction<'tcx>>,
786	    /// External device function declarations collected (for FFI with external LTOIR).
787	    device_externs: Vec<DeviceExternDecl>,
788	    /// DefIds of device externs already seen (prevents duplicates).
789	    seen_device_externs: HashSet<DefId>,
790	    /// Whether we've already emitted the "`DynamicSharedArray::get`
791	    /// needs `shared_mem_bytes` set at launch" warning. Fired at most
792	    /// once per program — the message is procedural advice about the
793	    /// kernel-launch contract, not anything per-call.
794	    warned_dynamic_shared_array: bool,
795	    /// Print progress to stderr.
796	    verbose: bool,
797	}
798	
799	impl<'tcx> DeviceCollector<'tcx> {
800	    fn new(tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, verbose: bool) -> Self {
801	        Self {
802	            tcx,
803	            seen: HashSet::new(),
804	            used_export_names: HashSet::new(),
805	            worklist: VecDeque::new(),
806	            discovery: HashMap::new(),
807	            result: Vec::new(),
808	            device_externs: Vec::new(),
809	            seen_device_externs: HashSet::new(),
810	            warned_dynamic_shared_array: false,
811	            verbose,
812	        }
813	    }
814	
815	    /// Returns the fully qualified domain name (FQDN) for an instance.
816	    ///
817	    /// `def_path_str()` normally omits the crate name for local items (e.g. returns
818	    /// `cuda_oxide_device_<hash>_vecadd` instead of
819	    /// `helper_fn::cuda_oxide_device_<hash>_vecadd`).
820	    /// This method asks rustc's printer to resolve crate names exactly like
821	    /// `rustc_public`, ensuring that call sites and definitions use identical
822	    /// strings before lowering converts `::` to `__`.
823	    ///
824	    /// Use `with_no_trimmed_paths!` because rustc's display-oriented path
825	    /// printer can otherwise shorten concrete impl type arguments, e.g.
826	    /// `Wrapper::<Vec2>::dot_plus`, while stable MIR call sites use the full
827	    /// `Wrapper::<crate_name::Vec2>::dot_plus` spelling.
828	    fn fqdn(&self, instance: Instance<'tcx>) -> String {
829	        let def_id = instance.def_id();
830	        rustc_middle::ty::print::with_resolve_crate_name!(
831	            rustc_middle::ty::print::with_no_trimmed_paths!(
832	                self.tcx.def_path_str_with_args(def_id, instance.args)
833	            )
834	        )
835	    }
836	
837	    /// Adds a root function (kernel) to start collection from.
838	    fn add_root(&mut self, instance: Instance<'tcx>, is_kernel: bool, export_name: String) {
839	        // Use mangled name as the unique key - this distinguishes different
840	        // monomorphizations of the same generic function (e.g., map<f32, Closure1>
841	        // vs map<f32, Closure2>)
842	        let mangled = self.tcx.symbol_name(instance).name.to_string();
843	        if self.seen.insert(mangled.clone()) {
844	            // A root is its own provenance: diagnostics fall back to its
845	            // definition site until a more precise user-code call site is
846	            // recorded along a discovery edge.
847	            self.discovery.insert(
848	                mangled,
849	                DiscoveryCtx {
850	                    root_name: export_name.clone(),
851	                    root_is_kernel: is_kernel,
852	                    user_span: self.tcx.def_span(instance.def_id()),
853	                },
854	            );
855	            self.used_export_names.insert(export_name.clone());
856	            self.worklist.push_back(CollectedFunction {
857	                instance,
858	                is_kernel,
859	                export_name,
860	            });
861	        }
862	    }
863	
864	    /// Runs collection to completion, returning all discovered functions and extern declarations.
865	    fn collect(mut self) -> CollectionResult<'tcx> {
866	        while let Some(func) = self.worklist.pop_front() {
867	            let def_id = func.instance.def_id();
868	
869	            // Look up where this function was discovered from. Every
870	            // enqueued function gets an entry; the fallback only guards
871	            // against future call paths that forget to record one.
872	            let mangled = self.tcx.symbol_name(func.instance).name.to_string();
873	            let ctx = self
874	                .discovery
875	                .get(&mangled)
876	                .cloned()
877	                .unwrap_or_else(|| DiscoveryCtx {
878	                    root_name: func.export_name.clone(),
879	                    root_is_kernel: func.is_kernel,
880	                    user_span: self.tcx.def_span(def_id),
881	                });
882	
883	            // Get MIR body if available
884	            if self.tcx.is_mir_available(def_id) {
885	                // Use instance_mir for monomorphized MIR.
886	                // This returns OPTIMIZED MIR (post -C opt-level passes).
887	                let mir = self.tcx.instance_mir(func.instance.def);
888	
889	                if self.verbose {
890	                    eprintln!(
891	                        "[collector] Processing {} ({} basic blocks)",
892	                        func.export_name,
893	                        mir.basic_blocks.len()
894	                    );
895	                }
896	
897	                // Fail fast with an actionable diagnostic when this body
898	                // contains panic-formatting machinery the device pipeline
899	                // cannot compile (issue #76).
900	                self.check_panic_machinery(mir, &func, &ctx);
901	
902	                // Walk all basic blocks looking for calls.
903	                // Pass the caller so we can substitute its args into callees
904	                // and attribute diagnostics to the right discovery path.
905	                for bb_data in mir.basic_blocks.iter() {
906	                    if let Some(ref terminator) = bb_data.terminator {
907	                        self.process_terminator(terminator, mir, &func, &ctx);
908	                    }
909	                }
910	            }
911	
912	            self.result.push(func);
913	        }
914	
915	        CollectionResult {
916	            functions: self.result,
917	            device_externs: self.device_externs,
918	        }
919	    }
920	
921	    /// Adds an external device function declaration (for FFI with external LTOIR).
922	    fn add_device_extern(&mut self, def_id: DefId, full_name: &str) {
923	        // Skip if already seen
924	        if !self.seen_device_externs.insert(def_id) {
925	            return;
926	        }
927	
928	        // Extract the original function name (strip the prefix)
929	        // The #[link_name] attribute on the extern fn has the original name.
930	        // `device_extern_base_name` returns the part after DEVICE_EXTERN_PREFIX
931	        // and works for both bare and FQDN forms.
932	        let export_name = device_extern_base_name(full_name)
933	            .map(str::to_string)
934	            .unwrap_or_else(|| full_name.to_string());
935	
936	        // Extract NVVM attributes from the function's attributes
937	        let attrs = self.extract_device_extern_attrs(def_id);
938	
939	        if self.verbose {
940	            eprintln!(
941	                "[collector] Found device extern: {} (convergent={}, pure={}, readonly={})",
942	                export_name, attrs.is_convergent, attrs.is_pure, attrs.is_readonly
943	            );
944	        }
945	
946	        self.device_externs.push(DeviceExternDecl {
947	            def_id,
948	            export_name,
949	            attrs,
950	        });
951	    }
952	
953	    /// Extract NVVM attributes from a device extern function's rustc attributes.
954	    fn extract_device_extern_attrs(&self, def_id: DefId) -> DeviceExternAttrs {
955	        use rustc_span::Symbol;
956	        let mut attrs = DeviceExternAttrs::default();
957	
958	        let check = |name| {
959	            self.tcx
960	                .get_attrs_by_path(def_id, &[Symbol::intern(name)])
961	                .next()
962	                .is_some()
963	        };
964	        attrs.is_convergent = check("convergent");
965	        attrs.is_pure = check("pure");
966	        attrs.is_readonly = check("readonly");
967	
968	        attrs
969	    }
970	
971	    /// Process a terminator to find function calls.
972	    ///
973	    /// `caller` is the collected function containing this terminator.
974	    /// We use its instance args to substitute into callee args when the
975	    /// caller is generic, and its discovery context (`ctx`) to attribute
976	    /// diagnostics to the right kernel and user-code span.
977	    fn process_terminator(
978	        &mut self,
979	        terminator: &rustc_middle::mir::Terminator<'tcx>,
980	        mir: &rustc_middle::mir::Body<'tcx>,
981	        caller: &CollectedFunction<'tcx>,
982	        ctx: &DiscoveryCtx,
983	    ) {
984	        if let TerminatorKind::Call { func, .. } = &terminator.kind {
985	            // The span of the whole call expression, mapped back through
986	            // MIR inlining and macro expansion to the line the user wrote.
987	            // (The function operand's own span is reset to a dummy by MIR
988	            // inlining, so it is useless for diagnostics.)
989	            let call_span = outermost_user_span(mir, terminator.source_info);
990	            self.process_call_operand(func, call_span, caller, ctx);
991	        }
992	    }
993	
994	    /// Process a call operand to extract and add the callee.
995	    ///
996	    /// This is where we enforce the `no_std` requirement for device code.
997	    /// If the call target is from a forbidden crate (std, alloc, etc.),
998	    /// we panic with a clear error message.
999	    ///
1000	    /// `caller` is the collected function containing this call, used to
1001	    /// substitute its generic args into the callee's args when needed.
1002	    /// `ctx` is the caller's discovery provenance, used for diagnostics.
1003	    fn process_call_operand(
1004	        &mut self,
1005	        func: &rustc_middle::mir::Operand<'tcx>,
1006	        call_span: Span,
1007	        caller: &CollectedFunction<'tcx>,
1008	        ctx: &DiscoveryCtx,
1009	    ) {
1010	        use rustc_middle::mir::Operand;
1011	        use rustc_middle::ty::EarlyBinder;
1012	
1013	        let Operand::Constant(const_op) = func else {
1014	            return;
1015	        };
1016	
1017	        let ty = const_op.const_.ty();
1018	        let TyKind::FnDef(def_id, args) = ty.kind() else {
1019	            return;
1020	        };
1021	        let fn_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(*def_id);
1022	        if fn_path.contains("DynamicSharedArray")
1023	            && (fn_path.contains("::get")
1024	                || fn_path.contains("::get_raw")
1025	                || fn_path.contains("::offset"))
1026	            && !self.warned_dynamic_shared_array
1027	        {
1028	            self.warned_dynamic_shared_array = true;
1029	            self.tcx.sess.dcx().span_warn(
1030	                const_op.span,
1031	                "`DynamicSharedArray` returns CUDA dynamic shared memory; make sure the kernel launch config provides enough `shared_mem_bytes`",
1032	            );
1033	        }
1034	
1035	        // CRITICAL: Substitute the caller's args into the callee's args.
1036	        //
1037	        // When walking the MIR of a generic function like
1038	        // `cuda_oxide_kernel_<hash>_scale<T>`, calls to other functions may have
1039	        // generic args like `[T]`. We substitute the caller's concrete args
1040	        // (e.g., `[f32]`) to get the actual monomorphized callee.
1041	        //
1042	        // Example:
1043	        //   Caller: cuda_oxide_kernel_<hash>_scale::<f32> (args = [f32])
1044	        //   Call in MIR: scale<T>(...)  (args = [T])
1045	        //   After substitution: scale::<f32> (args = [f32])
1046	        let args = self.tcx.instantiate_and_normalize_erasing_regions(
1047	            caller.instance.args,
1048	            TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized(),
1049	            EarlyBinder::bind(*args),
1050	        );
1051	
1052	        // Check if function is from a crate we should compile
1053	        match self.should_collect_from_crate(*def_id) {
1054	            CollectDecision::Collect => {
1055	                // Continue processing below
1056	            }
1057	            CollectDecision::SkipIntentional => {
1058	                // Silently skip (fmt, panicking, etc.)
1059	                return;
1060	            }
1061	            CollectDecision::Forbidden {
1062	                crate_name,
1063	                fn_path,
1064	            } => {
1065	                // ERROR: Device code is calling a forbidden crate!
1066	                // Build a formatted error box (68 char inner width)
1067	                let border = "═".repeat(68);
1068	                let empty_line = format!("║{:68}║", "");
1069	
1070	                // Truncate fn_path if too long (max 48 chars to fit in box)
1071	                let fn_display = if fn_path.len() > 48 {
1072	                    format!("{}...", &fn_path[..45])
1073	                } else {
1074	                    fn_path.clone()
1075	                };
1076	
1077	                // Build the "From crate" line with proper padding
1078	                let crate_line = format!("║ From crate: '{}'", crate_name);
1079	                let crate_padded = format!("{:<69}║", crate_line);
1080	
1081	                // Build the last line with proper padding
1082	                let last_line = format!(
1083	                    "║ The '{}' crate cannot run on GPU (requires OS/heap).",
1084	                    crate_name
1085	                );
1086	                let last_padded = format!("{:<69}║", last_line);
1087	
1088	                panic!(
1089	                    "\n\n\
1090	╔{border}╗
1091	║{title:^68}║
1092	╠{border}╣
1093	║ Device code calls: {fn:<48}║
1094	{crate_line}
1095	{empty}
1096	║ Only these crates are allowed in device code:                      ║
1097	║   • Local crate (your kernel code)                                 ║
1098	║   • cuda_device (GPU intrinsics)                                     ║
1099	║   • core (no_std standard library)                                 ║
1100	{empty}
1101	{last_line}
1102	╚{border}╝
1103	\n",
1104	                    border = border,
1105	                    title = "CUDA-OXIDE: FORBIDDEN CRATE IN DEVICE CODE",
1106	                    fn = fn_display,
1107	                    crate_line = crate_padded,
1108	                    empty = empty_line,
1109	                    last_line = last_padded,
1110	                );
1111	            }
1112	        }
1113	
1114	        // Derive the discovery provenance for whatever this call edge leads
1115	        // to. While we are still inside user-written code (the caller is in
1116	        // the local crate), the call site itself is the most precise span we
1117	        // will ever have; `source_callsite()` walks macro expansions and
1118	        // MIR-inlined frames back to the line the user actually wrote. Once
1119	        // the walk has left user code (sysroot internals), keep the last
1120	        // user-code span we recorded.
1121	        let callee_ctx = DiscoveryCtx {
1122	            root_name: ctx.root_name.clone(),
1123	            root_is_kernel: ctx.root_is_kernel,
1124	            user_span: if caller.instance.def_id().is_local() && !call_span.is_dummy() {
1125	                call_span
1126	            } else {
1127	                ctx.user_span
1128	            },
1129	        };
1130	
1131	        // Callable-trait shims do not necessarily have a MIR body of their own.
1132	        // Identify the actual `Fn`, `FnMut`, or `FnOnce` trait through rustc's
1133	        // metadata, then collect only the trait's `Self` type (the receiver).
1134	        // Function-name matching is unsafe here: user functions may legally
1135	        // contain strings such as `call_once`.
1136	        let is_callable_trait_method = self
1137	            .tcx
1138	            .trait_of_assoc(*def_id)
1139	            .is_some_and(|trait_id| self.tcx.fn_trait_kind_from_def_id(trait_id).is_some());
1140	        if is_callable_trait_method
1141	            && let Some(receiver_ty) = args.iter().next().and_then(|arg| arg.as_type())
1142	        {
1143	            self.enqueue_callable_trait_receiver_body(receiver_ty, call_span, caller, &callee_ctx);
1144	            // Don't return - continue to try resolving the trait method too
1145	            // (even though it may fail, we still want to try).
1146	        }
1147	
1148	        // Try to resolve the instance with substitutions first, so we can
1149	        // check if we've already seen THIS specific monomorphization
1150	        let typing_env = TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized();
1151	        let Some(resolved) = Instance::try_resolve(self.tcx, typing_env, *def_id, args)
1152	            .ok()
1153	            .flatten()
1154	        else {
1155	            return;
1156	        };
1157	
1158	        // Skip already-seen monomorphizations (use mangled name as unique key)
1159	        let mangled = self.tcx.symbol_name(resolved).name.to_string();
1160	        if self.seen.contains(&mangled) {
1161	            return;
1162	        }
1163	
1164	        // Skip non-monomorphized instances (still have generic type parameters).
1165	        // This happens when walking the generic definition's MIR - the call args
1166	        // are still generic. We only want to collect concrete instantiations.
1167	        if !is_fully_monomorphized(self.tcx, resolved) {
1168	            if self.verbose {
1169	                eprintln!(
1170	                    "[collector] Skipping non-monomorphized callee: {}",
1171	                    self.tcx.def_path_str(resolved.def_id())
1172	                );
1173	            }
1174	            return;
1175	        }
1176	
1177	        // Skip intrinsics and other special functions
1178	        if !matches!(resolved.def, InstanceKind::Item(_)) {
1179	            return;
1180	        }
1181	
1182	        // Check if this is a device extern declaration (FFI with external LTOIR).
1183	        // These have no MIR body but should be emitted as LLVM `declare` statements.
1184	        let raw_name = self.tcx.def_path_str(resolved.def_id());
1185	        if is_device_extern_symbol(&raw_name) {
1186	            self.add_device_extern(resolved.def_id(), &raw_name);
1187	            return;
1188	        }
1189	
1190	        // Heap allocation guard (issue #108): every `Vec` / `Box` / `String`
1191	        // allocation funnels into the `__rust_alloc` shim family, which has
1192	        // no MIR body and no device-side implementation. Without this guard
1193	        // the walk silently continues into `alloc::` internals and the user
1194	        // eventually gets a constant-translation error spanned into
1195	        // `alloc/src/boxed.rs`. Fail here instead, at the first point where
1196	        // the allocator is provably reached from device code.
1197	        //
1198	        // Only sysroot functions can be the real allocator entry points. A
1199	        // user is free to define their own fn named `__rust_alloc` (the
1200	        // name is not reserved), and that compiles for the device like any
1201	        // other function, so local definitions must not trip the guard.
1202	        if !resolved.def_id().is_local()
1203	            && let Some(shim) = rust_alloc_shim_name(&raw_name)
1204	        {
1205	            self.report_heap_allocation(shim, caller, &callee_ctx);
1206	        }
1207	
1208	        if is_ptx_asm_marker_path(&raw_name) {
1209	            if self.verbose {
1210	                eprintln!("[collector] Skipping inline PTX marker: {raw_name}");
1211	            }
1212	            return;
1213	        }
1214	
1215	        if is_unroll_marker_path(&raw_name) {
1216	            if self.verbose {
1217	                eprintln!("[collector] Skipping unroll marker: {raw_name}");
1218	            }
1219	            return;
1220	        }
1221	
1222	        // Skip functions without MIR bodies (extern intrinsics like cuda_device::threadIdx_x).
1223	        // These are handled specially by the terminator translator in mir-importer
1224	        // which dispatches them to NVVM intrinsic operations.
1225	        if !self.tcx.is_mir_available(resolved.def_id()) {
1226	            if self.verbose {
1227	                eprintln!(
1228	                    "[collector] Skipping extern/intrinsic (no MIR): {}",
1229	                    self.tcx.def_path_str(resolved.def_id())
1230	                );
1231	            }
1232	            return;
1233	        }
1234	
1235	        // Check if it has an unreachable body (intrinsic placeholder)
1236	        if self.is_unreachable_body(resolved.def_id()) {
1237	            // Genuine intrinsic placeholders live in `cuda_device` and are
1238	            // rewritten to NVVM ops by the translator, so skipping them is
1239	            // correct. But a USER function can also collapse to a panic-only
1240	            // body, most commonly when a helper without `#[device]` calls
1241	            // `thread::index_1d()` and gets the host-only panicking stub
1242	            // inlined (issue #76). Silently skipping such a function leaves
1243	            // a dangling call that later fails module verification with
1244	            // "Symbol ... not found". Diagnose it here instead.
1245	            self.check_unreachable_callee(resolved, call_span, caller, &callee_ctx);
1246	            if self.verbose {
1247	                eprintln!(
1248	                    "[collector] Skipping intrinsic (unreachable body): {}",
1249	                    self.tcx.def_path_str(resolved.def_id())
1250	                );
1251	            }
1252	            return;
1253	        }
1254	
1255	        // Use FQDN so the export name matches what the MIR translator
1256	        // sees via `CrateDef::name()` on the call side.
1257	        let name = self.fqdn(resolved);
1258	        let export_name = self.compute_export_name(&name, resolved);
1259	
1260	        if self.verbose {
1261	            eprintln!("[collector] Discovered callee: {} -> {}", name, export_name);
1262	        }
1263	
1264	        self.discovery.insert(mangled.clone(), callee_ctx);
1265	        self.seen.insert(mangled);
1266	        self.worklist.push_back(CollectedFunction {
1267	            instance: resolved,
1268	            is_kernel: false,
1269	            export_name,
1270	        });
1271	    }
1272	
1273	    fn enqueue_callable_trait_receiver_body(
1274	        &mut self,
1275	        ty: Ty<'tcx>,
1276	        call_span: Span,
1277	        caller: &CollectedFunction<'tcx>,
1278	        ctx: &DiscoveryCtx,
1279	    ) {
1280	        let typing_env = TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized();
1281	        let (kind, instance) = match ty.kind() {
1282	            TyKind::Closure(closure_def_id, closure_substs) => {
1283	                let Some(instance) =
1284	                    Instance::try_resolve(self.tcx, typing_env, *closure_def_id, closure_substs)
1285	                        .ok()
1286	                        .flatten()
1287	                else {
1288	                    return;
1289	                };
1290	                ("closure", instance)
1291	            }
1292	            TyKind::FnDef(fn_def_id, fn_args) => {
1293	                let Some(instance) =
1294	                    Instance::try_resolve(self.tcx, typing_env, *fn_def_id, fn_args)
1295	                        .ok()
1296	                        .flatten()
1297	                else {
1298	                    return;
1299	                };
1300	                ("function item", instance)
1301	            }
1302	            _ => return,
1303	        };
1304	
1305	        match self.should_collect_from_crate(instance.def_id()) {
1306	            CollectDecision::Collect => {}
1307	            CollectDecision::SkipIntentional => {
1308	                let target = self.tcx.def_path_str(instance.def_id());
1309	                self.tcx
1310	                    .dcx()
1311	                    .struct_span_fatal(
1312	                        call_span,
1313	                        format!(
1314	                            "`{target}` cannot be used as a function item in device code because it requires special call-site lowering"
1315	                        ),
1316	                    )
1317	                    .with_help(
1318	                        "wrap the call in a local `#[device]` function and pass that wrapper instead",
1319	                    )
1320	                    .emit()
1321	            }
1322	            CollectDecision::Forbidden {
1323	                crate_name,
1324	                fn_path,
1325	            } => self
1326	                .tcx
1327	                .dcx()
1328	                .struct_span_fatal(
1329	                    call_span,
1330	                    format!(
1331	                        "device code cannot call function item `{fn_path}` from forbidden crate `{crate_name}`"
1332	                    ),
1333	                )
1334	                .with_note(format!(
1335	                    "the call is reachable from `{}`",
1336	                    ctx.root_name
1337	                ))
1338	                .emit(),
1339	        }
1340	
1341	        let mangled = self.tcx.symbol_name(instance).name.to_string();
1342	        if self.seen.contains(&mangled) {
1343	            return;
1344	        }
1345	        if !is_fully_monomorphized(self.tcx, instance) {
1346	            return;
1347	        }
1348	        if !matches!(instance.def, InstanceKind::Item(_)) {
1349	            return;
1350	        }
1351	        if !self.tcx.is_mir_available(instance.def_id()) {
1352	            return;
1353	        }
1354	        if self.is_unreachable_body(instance.def_id()) {
1355	            self.check_unreachable_callee(instance, call_span, caller, ctx);
1356	            return;
1357	        }
1358	
1359	        let name = self.fqdn(instance);
1360	        let export_name = self.compute_export_name(&name, instance);
1361	
1362	        if self.verbose {
1363	            eprintln!(
1364	                "[collector] Discovered {kind} body (via trait call): {name} -> {export_name}"
1365	            );
1366	        }
1367	
1368	        self.discovery.insert(mangled.clone(), ctx.clone());
1369	        self.seen.insert(mangled);
1370	        self.worklist.push_back(CollectedFunction {
1371	            instance,
1372	            is_kernel: false,
1373	            export_name,
1374	        });
1375	    }
1376	
1377	    /// Determines if a function from a given crate should be collected.
1378	    ///
1379	    /// Returns a [`CollectDecision`] indicating:
1380	    /// - `Collect`: Function should be collected for device compilation
1381	    /// - `SkipIntentional`: Skip silently (core::fmt, core::panicking)
1382	    /// - `Forbidden`: Error - function is from a forbidden crate (std, etc.)
1383	    ///
1384	    /// ## Kernel Entry Points (Cross-Crate Support)
1385	    ///
1386	    /// Kernels (detected via `is_kernel_symbol` from `reserved-oxide-symbols`)
1387	    /// are allowed from ANY crate. This enables library crates to export
1388	    /// generic kernels that get monomorphized when used in an application.
1389	    ///
1390	    /// ## Allowed Crates (for non-kernel callees)
1391	    ///
1392	    /// - Local crate: The user's kernel code
1393	    /// - `cuda_device`: Our GPU intrinsics library
1394	    /// - `core`: Standard library core (iterators, Option, etc.)
1395	    /// - `alloc`: Heap allocation (if user has configured a GPU allocator)
1396	    /// - Any crate reachable from a kernel (transitive closure)
1397	    ///
1398	    /// ## Intentionally Skipped
1399	    ///
1400	    /// - `core::fmt::*`: Format trait machinery uses function pointers
1401	    /// - `core::panicking::*`: Panic handling (handled by panic=abort)
1402	    ///
1403	    /// ## Forbidden (Error)
1404	    ///
1405	    /// - `std`: OS, I/O, threads - can't run on GPU
1406	    fn should_collect_from_crate(&self, def_id: DefId) -> CollectDecision {
1407	        // Always collect from local crate
1408	        if def_id.krate == LOCAL_CRATE {
1409	            return CollectDecision::Collect;
1410	        }
1411	
1412	        let crate_name = self.tcx.crate_name(def_id.krate);
1413	        let name_str = crate_name.as_str();
1414	
1415	        // The `libm` crate (glam's `nostd-libm` float backend) is intercepted at
1416	        // every call site by mir-importer's float-math dispatch and lowered to
1417	        // libdevice intrinsics (`__nv_sqrtf`, `__nv_sinf`, ...). Its bodies must
1418	        // therefore NOT be collected: translating libm's generic software-float
1419	        // implementations (e.g. `libm::math::generic::sqrt::sqrt_round`) would
1420	        // both be wasted work and trip importer gaps. Skip the whole crate; any
1421	        // libm function we don't yet intercept will surface as a missing symbol,
1422	        // signalling that `from_libm_path` needs another entry.
1423	        if name_str == "libm" {
1424	            return CollectDecision::SkipIntentional;
1425	        }
1426	
1427	        // Check if this is a kernel entry point. Kernels can come from ANY
1428	        // crate — this enables library crates to export generic kernels that
1429	        // get monomorphized when used in an application.
1430	        let fn_name = self.tcx.item_name(def_id);
1431	        if is_kernel_symbol(fn_name.as_str()) {
1432	            return CollectDecision::Collect;
1433	        }
1434	
1435	        // Forbidden crate: std (OS, I/O, threads) - absolutely can't run on GPU
1436	        if name_str == "std" {
1437	            let fn_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(def_id);
1438	            // A handful of `std::sys::cmath::*` libm shims are intercepted
1439	            // by mir-importer's float-math intrinsic dispatch and lowered
1440	            // directly to libdevice (`__nv_atan2f` etc.). They never enter
1441	            // device codegen, so silently skip them here instead of tripping
1442	            // the std-crate guard. This is what makes `f32::atan2`,
1443	            // `f32::atan`, and the f64 counterparts usable from device code
1444	            // (MIR-opt inlines the `#[inline]` `std` wrapper, leaving a
1445	            // direct call to the cmath shim at the kernel call site).
1446	            if is_intrinsic_lowered_cmath_shim(&fn_path) {
1447	                return CollectDecision::SkipIntentional;
1448	            }
1449	            return CollectDecision::Forbidden {
1450	                crate_name: name_str.to_string(),
1451	                fn_path,
1452	            };
1453	        }
1454	
1455	        // Allowed external crates (no_std compatible)
1456	        // - core: iterators, Option, Result, traits, etc.
1457	        // - alloc: Vec, Box, String (if user has GPU allocator)
1458	        // - cuda_device: our GPU intrinsics
1459	        let allowed = matches!(name_str, "core" | "alloc" | "cuda_device" | "cuda-device");
1460	
1461	        if !allowed {
1462	            // For other external crates, we allow them if they're reachable from a kernel.
1463	            // This enables cross-crate device functions. The key safety is:
1464	            // 1. std is explicitly forbidden above
1465	            // 2. Any crate that uses std won't compile to PTX anyway (missing symbols)
1466	            // 3. User gets a clear link error if they try to use incompatible code
1467	            //
1468	            // This permissive approach enables:
1469	            // - Library crates with device helper functions
1470	            // - Math libraries (e.g., libm, num-traits)
1471	            // - Custom device abstractions
1472	            if self.verbose {
1473	                let fn_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(def_id);
1474	                eprintln!(
1475	                    "[collector] Allowing function from external crate '{}': {}",
1476	                    name_str, fn_path
1477	                );
1478	            }
1479	            return CollectDecision::Collect;
1480	        }
1481	
1482	        // Filter out problematic modules (intentional skip, not an error)
1483	        let path = self.tcx.def_path_str(def_id);
1484	
1485	        // Skip formatting and panic machinery (uses FnPtr types we can't translate)
1486	        if path.contains("::fmt::") || path.contains("::panicking::") {
1487	            return CollectDecision::SkipIntentional;
1488	        }
1489	
1490	        // Skip precondition_check functions - these are UB check assertions that use
1491	        // string types for panic messages. Since our queries return false for
1492	        // RuntimeChecks(UbChecks), these functions are never actually called at runtime.
1493	        // Example: core::num::<impl usize>::unchecked_sub::precondition_check
1494	        if path.contains("precondition_check") {
1495	            return CollectDecision::SkipIntentional;
1496	        }
1497	
1498	        // NOTE: We no longer skip arithmetic trait methods (Mul::mul, Add::add, etc.)
1499	        // These become device functions with call overhead, but that's a separate
1500	        // optimization issue (forced-inline on monomorphic small bodies).
1501	        //
1502	        // Legacy mangled names (from prebuilt sysroot) contain $ characters which are
1503	        // invalid PTX identifiers. We sanitize these in compute_export_name().
1504	
1505	        CollectDecision::Collect
1506	    }
1507	
1508	    /// Computes the export name for a function.
1509	    ///
1510	    /// `name` must be the FQDN (from [`fqdn()`]) so that non-generic export names
1511	    /// match what `CrateDef::name()` returns on the call side. Both sides feed
1512	    /// the FQDN through pliron's `Legaliser`, which replaces every
1513	    /// non-`[A-Za-z0-9]` character with `_`. We return the *raw* FQDN here so
1514	    /// that the call-side legaliser and the export-side legaliser (in
1515	    /// `body.rs::translate_body`) see the same input string and resolve to the
1516	    /// same canonical identifier. Pre-legalising here would alias to a
1517	    /// different input string and trigger spurious dedupe suffixes.
1518	    ///
1519	    /// For resolved instances that still carry generic args, or for raw-name
1520	    /// collisions, we fall back to the mangled symbol name since the MIR
1521	    /// translator uses `Instance::mangled_name()` for the matching call sites.
1522	    /// Concrete impl paths that merely contain characters like `<`, `>`, and
1523	    /// `::` remain raw FQDNs and are legalized later on both sides.
1524	    fn compute_export_name(&mut self, name: &str, instance: Instance<'tcx>) -> String {
1525	        // CRITICAL: If the instance has generic args, we MUST use mangled name.
1526	        // The MIR translator uses mangled names for generic function calls
1527	        // (see terminator/mod.rs::extract_func_info), so we must match that here.
1528	        // Without this, the call site uses "_RINv...mapf..." but we export as "map".
1529	        let has_generic_args = !instance.args.is_empty();
1530	
1531	        let simple_name = name.to_string();
1532	
1533	        if has_generic_args || self.used_export_names.contains(&simple_name) {
1534	            // Use mangled symbol name to avoid conflicts.
1535	            // This handles generics (e.g., ptr::add::<i32>) and name collisions.
1536	            let mangled = self.tcx.symbol_name(instance).name.to_string();
1537	
1538	            // Sanitize for PTX: replace $ with _ (legacy mangling uses $LT$, $GT$, etc.)
1539	            let sanitized = sanitize_ptx_name(&mangled);
1540	
1541	            self.used_export_names.insert(sanitized.clone());
1542	            sanitized
1543	        } else {
1544	            self.used_export_names.insert(simple_name.clone());
1545	            simple_name
1546	        }
1547	    }
1548	
1549	    /// Checks if a function body is just `unreachable!()` (intrinsic placeholder).
1550	    ///
1551	    /// cuda_device intrinsics have placeholder bodies that panic when called on host:
1552	    ///
1553	    /// ```rust,ignore
1554	    /// pub fn threadIdx_x() -> u32 {
1555	    ///     unreachable!("threadIdx_x called outside CUDA kernel context")
1556	    /// }
1557	    /// ```
1558	    ///
1559	    /// These are translated specially to PTX intrinsics by the MIR translator.
1560	    /// We skip collecting them because their panic bodies would pull in `FnPtr`
1561	    /// types we can't handle.
1562	    fn is_unreachable_body(&self, def_id: DefId) -> bool {
1563	        if !self.tcx.is_mir_available(def_id) {
1564	            return false;
1565	        }
1566	
1567	        let mir = self.tcx.optimized_mir(def_id);
1568	
1569	        // Quick check: intrinsic bodies are very small (1-2 blocks)
1570	        if mir.basic_blocks.len() > 2 {
1571	            return false;
1572	        }
1573	
1574	        // Check for panic calls
1575	        for bb_data in mir.basic_blocks.iter() {
1576	            let Some(ref terminator) = bb_data.terminator else {
1577	                continue;
1578	            };
1579	            match &terminator.kind {
1580	                TerminatorKind::Call { func, .. } => {
1581	                    if let Some(callee_def_id) = self.get_call_def_id(func) {
1582	                        let path = self.tcx.def_path_str(callee_def_id);
1583	                        // Match panic functions from both core (no_std) and std:
1584	                        // - core::panicking::* (no_std mode)
1585	                        // - std::rt::panic_fmt (std mode - unreachable!() expands to this)
1586	                        if is_panic_entry_path(&path) {
1587	                            return true;
1588	                        }
1589	                    }
1590	                }
1591	                TerminatorKind::Unreachable => {}
1592	                _ => return false,
1593	            }
1594	        }
1595	
1596	        false
1597	    }
1598	
1599	    /// Emits the heap-allocation diagnostic (issue #108) and aborts.
1600	    ///
1601	    /// `shim` is the allocator entry point that was reached, `caller` is
1602	    /// the collected function whose body contains the call, and `ctx`
1603	    /// carries the originating root and the nearest user-code span.
1604	    fn report_heap_allocation(
1605	        &self,
1606	        shim: &str,
1607	        caller: &CollectedFunction<'tcx>,
1608	        ctx: &DiscoveryCtx,
1609	    ) -> ! {
1610	        let caller_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(caller.instance.def_id());
1611	        let root_kind = if ctx.root_is_kernel {
1612	            "kernel"
1613	        } else {
1614	            "device function"
1615	        };
1616	        self.tcx
1617	            .dcx()
1618	            .struct_span_fatal(
1619	                ctx.user_span,
1620	                "heap allocation is not supported in kernels (no device allocator); \
1621	                 use fixed-size arrays or SharedArray",
1622	            )
1623	            .with_note(format!(
1624	                "device code starting at {root_kind} `{}` reaches the Rust allocator \
1625	                 entry point `{shim}` through `{caller_path}`",
1626	                ctx.root_name
1627	            ))
1628	            .with_note(
1629	                "`Vec`, `Box`, `String`, and everything else that allocates relies on a \
1630	                 global heap allocator, which does not exist on the GPU",
1631	            )
1632	            .with_help(
1633	                "store the data in a fixed-size array `[T; N]` instead, or in a \
1634	                 `SharedArray` when the scratch space should be shared by the thread block",
1635	            )
1636	            .emit()
1637	    }
1638	
1639	    /// Diagnoses a callee whose entire body is panic machinery (issue #76).
1640	    ///
1641	    /// Reached from the `is_unreachable_body` skip in
1642	    /// [`process_call_operand`]. Genuine intrinsic placeholders (the
1643	    /// `cuda_device` stubs the translator rewrites by name) must keep
1644	    /// being skipped silently, so this only reports two specific cases:
1645	    ///
1646	    /// 1. The body contains the `thread::index_*` stub panic message.
1647	    ///    That message can only end up in device-reachable code when a
1648	    ///    helper without `#[device]` called the public stub, so the fix
1649	    ///    (annotate the helper) is reported with certainty.
1650	    /// 2. The callee is a user-crate function with a normal return type.
1651	    ///    Skipping it would leave a call to a symbol that is never
1652	    ///    defined, which later fails module verification with an opaque
1653	    ///    "Symbol ... not found" error.
1654	    ///
1655	    /// Functions that are declared diverging (return type `!`) are left
1656	    /// alone: their call sites have no target block, so the translator
1657	    /// already lowers them to LLVM `unreachable` (note: NOT a trap;
1658	    /// the optimizer may delete paths that provably reach it, a known
1659	    /// gap tracked separately).
1660	    fn check_unreachable_callee(
1661	        &self,
1662	        resolved: Instance<'tcx>,
1663	        call_span: Span,
1664	        caller: &CollectedFunction<'tcx>,
1665	        ctx: &DiscoveryCtx,
1666	    ) {
1667	        let def_id = resolved.def_id();
1668	        let mir = self.tcx.optimized_mir(def_id);
1669	
1670	        // Look for the index-stub panic message in the callee's body.
1671	        let mut scan = StrConstScan {
1672	            tcx: self.tcx,
1673	            found: Vec::new(),
1674	        };
1675	        scan.visit_body(mir);
1676	        let marker = scan
1677	            .found
1678	            .iter()
1679	            .find(|(_, _, text)| text.contains(MISSING_DEVICE_STUB_MARKER));
1680	
1681	        let caller_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(caller.instance.def_id());
1682	        let callee_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(def_id);
1683	        let user_call_span = call_span;
1684	        let root_kind = if ctx.root_is_kernel {
1685	            "kernel"
1686	        } else {
1687	            "device function"
1688	        };
1689	
1690	        if let Some((_, _, text)) = marker {
1691	            let stub = stub_name_from_marker_message(text);
1692	            let crate_name = self.tcx.crate_name(def_id.krate);
1693	            let is_cuda_device = matches!(crate_name.as_str(), "cuda_device" | "cuda-device");
1694	            let message = format!(
1695	                "`{stub}` only works inside `#[kernel]` / `#[device]` functions; \
1696	                 here it resolves to a host-only stub that panics instead of \
1697	                 reading the thread index"
1698	            );
1699	            if is_cuda_device {
1700	                // The collected caller invokes the public stub directly:
1701	                // the macros never rewrote this call site, so the caller
1702	                // itself is the function missing the annotation.
1703	                self.tcx
1704	                    .dcx()
1705	                    .struct_span_fatal(user_call_span, message)
1706	                    .with_note(format!(
1707	                        "`{caller_path}` is not annotated, so the macro rewrite that \
1708	                         turns `{stub}` into the device intrinsic never ran on this call"
1709	                    ))
1710	                    .with_help(format!(
1711	                        "annotate `{caller_path}` with `#[device]`, or compute the index \
1712	                         in the kernel and pass it in as a parameter"
1713	                    ))
1714	                    .emit()

1717	                // point at the helper definition and the device call site.
1718	                self.tcx
1719	                    .dcx()
1720	                    .struct_span_fatal(self.tcx.def_span(def_id), message)
1721	                    .with_span_note(
1722	                        user_call_span,
1723	                        format!(
1724	                            "`{callee_path}` is called from device code here \
1725	                             (reached from {root_kind} `{}`)",
1726	                            ctx.root_name
1727	                        ),
1728	                    )
1729	                    .with_note(format!(
1730	                        "`{callee_path}` is not annotated, so the macro rewrite that \
1731	                         turns `{stub}` into the device intrinsic never ran inside it"
1732	                    ))
1733	                    .with_help(format!(
1734	                        "annotate `{callee_path}` with `#[device]`, or compute the index \
1735	                         in the kernel and pass it in as a parameter"
1736	                    ))

1738	            }
1739	        }
1740	
1741	        // No stub marker: leave non-local functions to the existing silent
1742	        // skip (this is what keeps the real `cuda_device` intrinsic
1743	        // placeholders and `core`'s cold panic wrappers working), and leave
1744	        // declared-diverging functions to the translator's `unreachable` lowering.

1747	        }

1749	        self.tcx
1750	            .dcx()
1751	            .struct_span_fatal(
1752	                self.tcx.def_span(def_id),
1753	                format!(
1754	                    "`{callee_path}` is called from device code but its body is \
1755	                     nothing but a panic, which cannot be compiled for the GPU"
1756	                ),
1757	            )
1758	            .with_span_note(
1759	                user_call_span,
1760	                format!(
1761	                    "called from device code here (reached from {root_kind} `{}`)",
1762	                    ctx.root_name
1763	                ),
1764	            )
1765	            .with_note(
1766	                "likely causes: a function used in device code without `#[device]` \
1767	                 (its body then resolves to a host-only stub that panics), or a \
1768	                 function that unconditionally panics",
1769	            )
1770	            .with_help(
1771	                "annotate device helpers with `#[device]`; if the panic is \
1772	                 intentional, declare the function as diverging (`-> !`) so the \
1773	                 call lowers to LLVM `unreachable`",
1774	            )
1775	            .emit()
1776	    }
1777	
1778	    /// Diagnoses panic-formatting machinery inside a collected body
1779	    /// (issue #76).
1780	    ///
1781	    /// Called from [`collect`] for every function that is about to be
1782	    /// translated. A basic block that ends in a call into
1783	    /// `core::panicking` and *materializes a `&str` constant in a
1784	    /// statement* (the panic message, or pieces of a `format_args!`
1785	    /// template) cannot be translated: string constants in statements have
1786	    /// no device lowering, so the user would get an opaque
1787	    /// constant-translation error pointing into `core`. Report the real
1788	    /// situation instead.
1789	    ///
1790	    /// Panic calls whose message travels only in the call arguments are
1791	    /// left alone on purpose: diverging call arguments are dropped by the
1792	    /// translator and the call lowers to LLVM `unreachable`, which is the
1793	    /// supported behavior for conditional panics like `unwrap`.
1794	    fn check_panic_machinery(
1795	        &self,
1796	        mir: &rustc_middle::mir::Body<'tcx>,
1797	        func: &CollectedFunction<'tcx>,
1798	        ctx: &DiscoveryCtx,
1799	    ) {
1800	        for (bb, bb_data) in mir.basic_blocks.iter_enumerated() {
1801	            let Some(term) = &bb_data.terminator else {
1802	                continue;
1803	            };
1804	            let TerminatorKind::Call { func: callee, .. } = &term.kind else {
1805	                continue;
1806	            };
1807	            let Some(callee_did) = self.get_call_def_id(callee) else {
1808	                continue;
1809	            };
1810	            if !is_panic_entry_path(&self.tcx.def_path_str(callee_did)) {
1811	                continue;
1812	            }
1813	
1814	            // Collect the string constants feeding this panic block.
1815	            let mut scan = StrConstScan {
1816	                tcx: self.tcx,
1817	                found: Vec::new(),
1818	            };
1819	            scan.visit_basic_block_data(bb, bb_data);
1820	
1821	            let func_path = self.tcx.def_path_str(func.instance.def_id());
1822	            let root_kind = if ctx.root_is_kernel {
1823	                "kernel"
1824	            } else {
1825	                "device function"
1826	            };
1827	            // When the panic sits directly in the root's body, naming the
1828	            // (mangled) containing function adds nothing; name the root once.
1829	            let location_note = if func.export_name == ctx.root_name {
1830	                format!(
1831	                    "the panic is inside the body of {root_kind} `{}`",
1832	                    ctx.root_name
1833	                )
1834	            } else {
1835	                format!(
1836	                    "the panic lives in `{func_path}`, reached from {root_kind} `{}`",
1837	                    ctx.root_name
1838	                )
1839	            };
1840	
1841	            // Best user-facing span: the panic call site, mapped back
1842	            // through MIR inlining and macro expansion to user code.
1843	            let user_span = outermost_user_span(mir, term.source_info);
1844	
1845	            // Stub marker: the `thread::index_*` stub was inlined into this
1846	            // body, so a function on the way here is missing `#[device]`.
1847	            if let Some((_, _, text)) = scan
1848	                .found
1849	                .iter()
1850	                .find(|(_, _, text)| text.contains(MISSING_DEVICE_STUB_MARKER))
1851	            {
1852	                let stub = stub_name_from_marker_message(text);
1853	                self.tcx
1854	                    .dcx()
1855	                    .struct_span_fatal(
1856	                        user_span,
1857	                        format!(
1858	                            "`{stub}` only works inside `#[kernel]` / `#[device]` \
1859	                             functions; here it resolves to a host-only stub that \
1860	                             panics instead of reading the thread index"
1861	                        ),
1862	                    )
1863	                    .with_note(location_note.clone())
1864	                    .with_help(format!(
1865	                        "annotate the helper that calls `{stub}` with `#[device]`, or \
1866	                         compute the index in the kernel and pass it in as a parameter"
1867	                    ))
1868	                    .emit()
1869	            }
1870	
1871	            // A panic message materialized in a statement: translation of
1872	            // this body is guaranteed to fail, so error out with the likely
1873	            // causes instead.
1874	            if scan
1875	                .found
1876	                .iter()
1877	                .any(|(loc, _, _)| loc.statement_index < bb_data.statements.len())
1878	            {
1879	                self.tcx
1880	                    .dcx()
1881	                    .struct_span_fatal(
1882	                        user_span,
1883	                        "device code reaches a panic that builds a message string; \
1884	                         panic message formatting is not supported on the GPU",
1885	                    )
1886	                    .with_note(location_note)
1887	                    .with_note(
1888	                        "likely causes: a function used in device code without \
1889	                         `#[device]` (its body then resolves to a host-only stub that \
1890	                         panics), or a real panic path such as `panic!` / `assert!` / \
1891	                         `expect` with a message",
1892	                    )
1893	                    .with_help(
1894	                        "annotate device helpers with `#[device]`; for intentional \
1895	                         checks, branch and return instead of panicking with a message",
1896	                    )
1897	                    .emit()
1898	            }
1899	        }
1900	    }
1901	
1902	    /// Extracts the DefId from a call operand.
1903	    fn get_call_def_id(&self, func: &rustc_middle::mir::Operand<'tcx>) -> Option<DefId> {
1904	        use rustc_middle::mir::Operand;
1905	
1906	        let Operand::Constant(const_op) = func else {
1907	            return None;
1908	        };
1909	
1910	        let ty = const_op.const_.ty();
1911	        if let TyKind::FnDef(def_id, _) = ty.kind() {
1912	            Some(*def_id)
1913	        } else {
1914	            None
1915	        }
1916	    }
1917	}
1918	
1919	/// Dumps MIR info for collected device functions.
1920	///
1921	/// This is useful for debugging to see what was collected and verify
1922	/// the MIR statistics (basic blocks, locals, args) look reasonable.
1923	pub fn dump_device_mir_info<'tcx>(tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, functions: &[CollectedFunction<'tcx>]) {
1924	    eprintln!("\n=== Device Functions MIR Info ===");
1925	    for func in functions {
1926	        let def_id = func.instance.def_id();
1927	        eprintln!(
1928	            "\n{} [{}]:",
1929	            func.export_name,
1930	            if func.is_kernel { "kernel" } else { "device" }
1931	        );
1932	
1933	        if tcx.is_mir_available(def_id) {
1934	            let mir = tcx.instance_mir(func.instance.def);
1935	            eprintln!("  - {} basic blocks", mir.basic_blocks.len());
1936	            eprintln!("  - {} local variables", mir.local_decls.len());
1937	            eprintln!("  - {} args", mir.arg_count);
1938	
1939	            // Show return type
1940	            let ret_ty = mir.local_decls[rustc_middle::mir::RETURN_PLACE].ty;
1941	            eprintln!("  - returns: {:?}", ret_ty);
1942	        } else {
1943	            eprintln!("  - MIR not available");
1944	        }
1945	    }
1946	    eprintln!("=================================\n");
1947	}

101	/// Output paths, target, and artifact format from successful compilation.
102	pub struct CompilationResult {

118	/// Configuration for the compilation pipeline.
119	pub struct PipelineConfig {
120	    /// Directory for output files (`.ll`, `.ptx`).
121	    pub output_dir: std::path::PathBuf,
122	    /// Base name for output files (e.g., `"kernel"` → `kernel.ll`, `kernel.ptx`).

131	    ///
132	    /// When true:
133	    /// - Uses full NVPTX datalayout
134	    /// - Adds `@llvm.used` to preserve kernels from optimization
135	    /// - Adds `!nvvm.annotations` for all kernels
136	    /// - Adds `!nvvmir.version` metadata
137	    /// - Outputs `.ll` file in NVVM IR format
138	    ///
139	    /// The output can be compiled to LTOIR using `nvvmCompileProgram -gen-lto`.
140	    ///
141	    /// Pre-Blackwell targets use the legacy LLVM 7 dialect; Blackwell and
142	    /// newer targets use the modern opaque-pointer dialect. Architecture is
143	    /// controlled by `target_arch` or `device_arch_hint` (normally populated
144	    /// by `cargo oxide`). When an ordinary build switches to NVVM IR after
145	    /// detecting libdevice, the pipeline may instead select the module's
146	    /// feature-based target floor.
